meta.note: Eastern Bloc sweep (Hungary, Romania, Czechoslovakia [code CS], Yugoslavia [code YU]). HU ban-adds: Darkness at Noon, Portnoy's Complaint, The Naked and the Dead. Held: poems (Márai Funeral Oration, Blandiana Totul), a likely duplicate (Müller Niederungen vs the held Nadirs), and a low-confidence YU plates-seizure case. Some Kádár-era ban years for foreign titles are proxies, noted.

entries.title: A Worker in a Worker's State
entries.description_ban: Factory exposé (Darabbér) whose manuscript was seized; Haraszti was prosecuted in 1973–74 for 'grave incitement' and a People's Court found it liable to provoke hatred of the state. Smuggled abroad.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific seizure and prosecution; documented by the JHI Blog.
entries.source_name: JHI Blog (Journal of the History of Ideas)

entries.title: The Intellectuals on the Road to Class Power
entries.description_ban: Police found the hidden manuscript in 1974 and arrested both authors for 'incitement against the state'; it was published only abroad.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific seizure; documented via Wikipedia with citations.

entries.title: The Velvet Prison
entries.description_ban: Essay on the aesthetics of state censorship (A cenzúra esztétikája) that could not be officially published in Kádár-era Hungary; circulated only as samizdat.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific samizdat-only suppression; documented by Eurozine.

entries.title: The Dukays
entries.description_ban: Copies were collected from stores, the final pages physically cut out with razor blades, and the mutilated books returned to shelves after the 1968 Prague events.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific physical censorship; documented by Hungarian Literature Online.

entries.title: Laughable Loves
entries.description_ban: Published in Hungary just before the Prague Spring, then recalled and pulled from stores after the Soviet-led crushing of the uprising.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific recall; documented by Hungarian Literature Online.

entries.title: The Misery of the Small Eastern European States
entries.description_ban: Bibó's works were entirely barred from publication under the one-party system from 1948; this treatise circulated only via samizdat.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban; documented by Britannica.

entries.title: Darkness at Noon
entries.description_ban: Koestler's name was taboo as a 'renegade Communist'; the novel could not be published officially in Kádár-era Hungary and appeared only via samizdat. Adds a Hungary ban to the existing record.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Adds a Hungary ban; documented by Hungarian Literature Online.

entries.title: Portnoy's Complaint
entries.description_ban: Rejected by Kádár-era publishing authorities as 'too strong for the regime's taste' and not allowed into print. Adds a Hungary ban to the existing record.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Adds a Hungary ban; documented by Hungarian Literature Online.

entries.title: The Naked and the Dead
entries.description_ban: Deemed undesirable by Kádár-era censors over its sexual content and language. Adds a Hungary ban to the existing record. Ban year approximate.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Adds a Hungary ban; documented by Hungarian Literature Online (medium confidence).

entries.title: Ostinato
entries.description_ban: Goma's prison-experience novel was refused by Romanian censors and instead published by Suhrkamp in West Germany in 1971; Goma was expelled from the party.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific refusal/ban; documented via Wikipedia.

entries.title: Gherla
entries.description_ban: Goma's novel about the Gherla prison was denied publication in Romania and published by Gallimard in Paris in 1976.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban; documented via Wikipedia.

entries.title: Noaptea de Sânziene
entries.description_ban: Eliade's novel (The Forbidden Forest) was unpublishable in Romania for two decades; in the 1960 Noica–Pillat trial, smuggled copies were entered as incriminating evidence. The Romanian text appeared only in Paris (1971).
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban; documented via Wikipedia.

entries.title: Așteptând ceasul de apoi
entries.description_ban: Pillat's manuscript novel was confiscated as 'legionary' evidence in the 1960 Noica–Pillat trial and believed destroyed; the surviving copy, found in the Securitate archives, was published only in 2010.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific confiscation/suppression; documented via Romanian Wikipedia.

entries.title: Drückender Tango
entries.description_ban: After this story collection depicting village misery, Müller was forbidden to publish again in Romania; she emigrated in 1987.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban; documented by Britannica.

entries.title: Întâmplări de pe strada mea
entries.description_ban: Blandiana's children's book, featuring the cat Arpagic read as a Ceaușescu caricature, was denounced as 'anti-political' and withdrawn from circulation. Exact year approximate.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific withdrawal; documented via CEEOL (medium confidence).

entries.title: The Cowards
entries.description_ban: Withdrawn from sale and copies destroyed weeks after release; the KSČ charged it with 'defamation of the anti-fascist resistance and denigration of the Red Army'.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific withdrawal; documented via Wikipedia.

entries.title: Buds
entries.description_ban: Already printed and bound, the entire 1970 print run (Poupata) was confiscated and pulped before distribution during early normalization.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific confiscation; documented via Wikipedia.

entries.title: Homework
entries.description_ban: Banned in 1970 after being printed and bound but before distribution, alongside Poupata (Buds).
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific confiscation; documented via Wikipedia.

entries.title: The Axe
entries.description_ban: Vaculík's last officially published novel (Sekyra); after 1968 he was barred from publishing and The Axe survived only in samizdat (Edice Petlice) and foreign translation.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific normalization-era ban; documented via Wikipedia.

entries.title: Life Is Elsewhere
entries.description_ban: Forbidden Czech publication and first issued in French (1974, Prix Médicis) while Kundera was blacklisted and his works pulled from libraries.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban; documented via Wikipedia.

entries.title: The Farewell Party
entries.description_ban: Written 1969–70 (Valčík na rozloučenou); banned in Czechoslovakia, first published in French (1976) and in Czech only via the exile house Sixty-Eight Publishers (1979).
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban; documented via Wikipedia.

entries.title: Judge on Trial
entries.description_ban: Klíma's fiction was banned after the 1968 invasion; Judge on Trial was first published underground in 1978 (samizdat) and only legally published in the early 1990s.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban; documented by Britannica.

entries.title: Love and Garbage
entries.description_ban: Written during Klíma's two-decade publishing ban; circulated in samizdat and abroad, unable to be legally published in Czechoslovakia until after 1989.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban; documented by Britannica.

entries.title: The Plague Column
entries.description_ban: The future Nobel laureate was largely barred from publishing in the 1970s; this collection (Morový sloup) appeared in samizdat (Edice Petlice, 1973) and in exile rather than officially.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific samizdat-only suppression; documented by Encyclopedia.com.

entries.title: The Hangwoman
entries.description_ban: Kohout's works were unpublished in Czechoslovakia 1968–1989; this dark satire (Katyně, written 1970) appeared only in samizdat and abroad.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban within Kohout's blanket publishing ban (medium confidence).

entries.title: Vunena vremena
entries.description_ban: Poetry collection (Woollen Times, Prosveta); the entire print run was destroyed in May 1981 and the poet tried for 'hostile propaganda' / defaming Tito's memory, convicted Feb 1982.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban and prosecution; documented via Wikipedia and the Christian Science Monitor.

entries.title: Moscow Summer 1964
entries.description_ban: Essay on a 1964 Soviet trip banned in Yugoslavia for 'slandering a friendly state'; published in the West, and the author imprisoned.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban; documented via Wikipedia and the COURAGE registry.

entries.title: Nož
entries.description_ban: Novel (The Knife) condemned by the Communist Party and banned for 'spreading national and religious hatred' and undermining brotherhood-and-unity; the author was expelled from the party.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban; documented via Wikipedia.

entries.title: Kad su cvetale tikve
entries.description_ban: Goli Otok-themed novel (When the Pumpkins Blossomed); after Tito personally denounced the 1969 stage adaptation, the novel was withdrawn from libraries and bookstores nationwide.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific withdrawal; documented by RTS.

entries.title: Frede, laku noć
entries.description_ban: Short-story collection withdrawn alongside Kad su cvetale tikve during the 1969 suppression of the author.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific withdrawal; documented by RTS and Serbian press (medium confidence).

entries.title: Golubnjača
entries.description_ban: Story collection / stage work on Ustashe crimes against Serbs; banned in Yugoslavia 1981–1984 as ethno-nationally provocative.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban; documented in academic literature (medium confidence; the ban centred on the dramatisation of the published stories).
